source.title: Dedicated Beta - Staged Rollouts check-in (gates + Console feedback)
source.summary: Beta check-in with Lattice AI on Dedicated staged rollouts. Customer likes the stage timeline concept and promotion audit trail, but reported friction around (1) gate strictness (p99 latency + 429/drop thresholds) and noisy failures during low-traffic periods, (2) confusion about what "saturation/headroom" means per stage, and (3) Console UX gaps: unclear gate evidence, limited per-region breakdown, and too many clicks to promote/hold/rollback. Redwood aligned on tuning: minimum sample size, better missing-metrics messaging, and UI improvements (gate evidence panel, annotations, and simplified promote confirmation).
topics: Beta status + recent rollout attempt (1% canary -> hold -> rollback)
Gate thresholds: latency, error rate, 429/drop, saturation/headroom, OOM
Telemetry lag / missing metrics and whether gates should fail closed
Dedicated pool partitioning impact on effective capacity at small canary sizes
Console UX: stage timeline, evidence drill-down, region breakdown, and audit log visibility
Overrides: hold vs abort vs force-promote, and customer/admin permissions
next_steps: Redwood to share proposed updated default gate thresholds (and rationale) for Dedicated beta pools by Mar 10.
Redwood to add Console UI copy/tooltips explaining saturation/headroom and how it’s computed per stage.
Lattice to provide timestamps + rollout_id for the last two gate failures (p99 + 429 spike) so Redwood can correlate with telemetry.
Schedule a 30-min follow-up after Redwood ships the gate evidence panel fix to confirm it resolves the “empty evidence” experience.
action_items: Logan Wright - Pull telemetry for Lattice rollout on 2025-03-03 ~19:10 UTC and confirm stage_id tagging and evidence query results - due 2025-03-07
Marissa Cole - Propose Console UX tweaks: one-page stage details view, region toggle, and fewer clicks to promote/hold - due 2025-03-11
Rafael Mendes - Review whether 429/drop gate should be evaluated against baseline (previous stage) vs absolute threshold in low-volume windows - due 2025-03-12
Lauren Bishop - Send beta guidance on recommended stage sizing for small pools (replica partitions vs percent) - due 2025-03-08
Alex Chen (Lattice) - Send desired gate configuration knobs (what must be configurable vs fixed) - due 2025-03-13
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-03-05
Time: 9:00am PT (recorded)
Duration: 47 min
Attendees (Redwood): Liam O'Rourke, Lauren Bishop, Marissa Cole, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright
Attendees (Lattice AI): Alex Chen, Priya Desai, Morgan Lee, Samir Patel

[00:00] Liam O'Rourke: Cool, alright. Recording is on. Thanks everyone for making time. This is our Dedicated beta staged rollouts check-in. Goal today is mostly: how did the promotion flow feel, where did the gates feel too strict or too loose, and what do we need to fix in Console before we expand this to more pools.

[00:17] Alex Chen: Yep. Sounds good.

[00:19] Lauren Bishop: Hey all.

[00:21] Marissa Cole: Hi.

[00:22] Priya Desai: Morning.

[00:25] Liam O'Rourke: Maybe quick context, Alex—last time we talked you were going to try moving your reranker pool to the new runtime build behind a 1% canary stage, right?

[00:34] Alex Chen: Yeah, we did. We did it Monday. It… mostly worked. The mechanics worked. But we got blocked on the gates pretty quickly.

[00:43] Liam O'Rourke: Ok. Want to walk us through what happened, and like, what you expected to see versus what you saw?

[00:49] Alex Chen: Sure. So we created the rollout plan with the default stages—1, 10, 50, 100. I think we left names as-is. We hit promote to 1%. Console showed it as “Running - Stage 1%” and then after… I want to say maybe 6 minutes, it flipped to “Gate failed”.

[01:09] Priya Desai: It actually said “p99 latency gate failed” and “429 gate failed”.

[01:14] Alex Chen: Right. It didn’t show us the numbers though. Like it said failed, but the evidence panel was empty for us.

[01:20] Marissa Cole: Empty as in the section didn’t render? Or it was like “no data”?

[01:24] Alex Chen: It showed the section but it was like blank rows. We tried refreshing. Same thing.

[01:30] Morgan Lee: I saw some spinner and then nothing. Like it never loaded.

[01:34] Logan Wright: Okay, that’s helpful. We had a known issue last week where stage tags were not consistent between traces and metrics. It might be that.

[01:43] Liam O'Rourke: So you got a fail state but no evidence explaining why.

[01:46] Alex Chen: Exactly. So the experience is like, okay do we trust it? Or do we force promote? And then the “force promote” button is kinda scary.

[01:56] Marissa Cole: Yeah. That’s fair.

[01:58] Liam O'Rourke: Priya, from SRE side, did you see anything in your own dashboards? Like was latency actually up, or was it noise?

[02:07] Priya Desai: Mixed. We saw p95 basically flat. p99 had spikes but we were at low volume in the canary. Like 1% of traffic is tiny for us overnight. So p99 is… not super stable.

[02:19] Rafael Mendes: That aligns with one of the concerns we had: tail latency gates and minimum sample size. What was your traffic rate at 1%? Roughly.

[02:28] Alex Chen: Uh depends by hour. Monday was like 250 requests per second peak for that pool. But 1% is 2.5 rps. And then if you’re using a five minute window that’s, what, 750 requests. But a lot of our calls are streaming and some are short, some are long.

[02:48] Morgan Lee: Also the reranker calls aren’t streaming. The chat is. Sorry.

[02:52] Alex Chen: Right, sorry, I mixed. This pool is embeddings + rerank, not streaming.

[02:56] Rafael Mendes: Still. 2.5 rps can be borderline for p99 gating depending on your distribution.

[03:02] Liam O'Rourke: And you also saw 429 failures at the 1% stage?

[03:07] Priya Desai: Yeah that’s the weird part. We got 429s even though we’re sending less traffic.

[03:12] Alex Chen: We think it’s because the canary partition is too small. Like if it’s carved out and it only has, say, one GPU or one replica, then you can get queueing and you can get rate limiting just in that partition.

[03:25] Logan Wright: Right, partitioned capacity. That can cause localized saturation even if overall pool has headroom.

[03:30] Lauren Bishop: For beta we’ve been recommending that 1% stage is traffic split, not replica split, unless the pool is small. But I know the underlying thing still has to map to actual replicas at some point.

[03:41] Alex Chen: Yeah. And we don’t know which one it did. Console didn’t say. We just saw “1%”.

[03:46] Marissa Cole: That’s a great point. We can show “effective capacity” or “estimated replicas” per stage.

[03:52] Liam O'Rourke: Okay, so two buckets: gate strictness and opacity.

[03:56] Samir Patel: And a third: the workflow. We had a hold state and we weren’t sure what it meant.

[04:02] Liam O'Rourke: Say more.

[04:04] Samir Patel: So after it failed, the UI showed “Hold recommended” or something? Or maybe I’m misremembering.

[04:10] Alex Chen: It showed “Rollout held” because we clicked hold. Sorry, we did that.

[04:14] Priya Desai: We held it because we didn’t want auto rollback to happen while we were looking.

[04:19] Rafael Mendes: Did it auto rollback for you at all?

[04:22] Priya Desai: Not automatically. It went into failed and then we had actions: hold, abort, rollback. Rollback button was confusing because we were already at stage 1%. Like rollback to what? 0?

[04:36] Marissa Cole: That’s on us. Internally we say rollback means “return traffic to baseline stage,” but if you’re at first stage it’s basically “disable canary”. We can rename.

[04:47] Liam O'Rourke: Yeah, language matters.

[04:49] Alex Chen: Also when it’s held, we couldn’t tell if traffic was still at 1% or if it paused changes.

[04:56] Lauren Bishop: Hold is supposed to freeze the stage, i.e., keep current split and stop automatic promotions/rollbacks. But it should be explicit in UI.

[05:06] Alex Chen: Exactly. Because we wanted to keep it at 1% while we investigated. But we weren’t certain.

[05:12] [crosstalk]
Marissa Cole: Mm-hmm.
Rafael Mendes: Yeah.

[05:15] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s dig into the thresholds question. Priya, you said p95 looked fine. p99 noisy. What would be a gate that feels reasonable for you?

[05:26] Priya Desai: For canary we’d rather gate on error rate, not p99. Or we’d like p99 to be relative to baseline not absolute. If baseline p99 is already, like, 400ms, and you set a hard 300ms threshold, we’ll always fail.

[05:42] Rafael Mendes: Our current default is absolute plus some headroom, depending on tier. But I agree relative-to-baseline is better for Dedicated because baselines vary by customer and by model.

[05:53] Alex Chen: Yeah. And then sample size. It should say “insufficient data” not “failed”. Because it’s not failed, it’s unknown.

[06:02] Logan Wright: We do have an “unknown” state internally but I think the UI is collapsing it.

[06:07] Marissa Cole: It currently shows as “Gate failed (missing data)” which is not ideal.

[06:11] Alex Chen: That’s what it felt like.

[06:14] Liam O'Rourke: There was also a “saturation/headroom” gate. Did that ever trip or did you look at it?

[06:21] Morgan Lee: I didn’t know what it meant. Like we saw a green check but no idea.

[06:26] Alex Chen: Yeah. We need an explanation of “saturation”. Is it GPU utilization? Is it queue depth? Is it token throughput?

[06:34] Logan Wright: It’s a composite right now: queue depth percentile and GPU duty cycle. But we should not hide that. We should show the components.

[06:44] Rafael Mendes: For SRE sanity, we try to avoid single metrics because people game them. But I agree if it’s composite you need a breakdown.

[06:52] Priya Desai: Also, it matters which stage you’re measuring. Like saturation in canary partition is expected to be higher. That’s not necessarily a reason to fail if overall is fine. Or you need different thresholds per stage size.

[07:08] Lauren Bishop: That’s a great callout. We have stage-size aware thresholds in the design doc, but we might not be applying them consistently yet.

[07:18] Liam O'Rourke: Another question: did any gates feel too lenient? Like you would have liked it to catch something but it didn’t.

[07:27] Alex Chen: Hard to say because we didn’t get past stage 1. But we did notice a quality issue in our eval prompts Tuesday, not sure if related.

[07:36] Morgan Lee: It wasn’t huge. Just some ranking shifts.

[07:40] Liam O'Rourke: Are you using Redwood’s quality regression gate at all, or is that off?

[07:46] Alex Chen: It was off. We don’t want to block rollout on it yet. But we’d like a warning banner maybe.

[07:53] Marissa Cole: Like “quality signal degraded” but not gating promotion.

[07:57] Alex Chen: Yeah.

[08:00] Liam O'Rourke: Okay. Let’s talk about per-region. Your Dedicated pool is multi-region, right? us-east and eu-west?

[08:07] Priya Desai: Yes.

[08:08] Alex Chen: And the UI was only showing aggregated gates. We need to see region breakdown because we had an issue where eu-west was fine but us-east had a spike.

[08:18] Logan Wright: We can generate gate evidence per region but we were worried about noise and clutter.

[08:24] Samir Patel: We’d prefer the clutter. Or at least a toggle.

[08:27] Marissa Cole: That’s consistent feedback from others too. We’re planning a region filter for stage evidence.

[08:33] Liam O'Rourke: Great. On the promote flow itself: how many clicks, did it feel safe, did it feel like change management?

[08:43] Alex Chen: It’s close. The confirmation modal is okay but we want the “what will happen” summary. Like: traffic goes from 1% to 10%, what’s the estimated replica allocation, what are the gates that will be evaluated, what time window, and what will auto abort do.

[09:03] Priya Desai: And we want to set a reason for promotion. Like a change ticket ID.

[09:08] Rafael Mendes: We do have a “reason” field in audit logs but it’s optional. We can make it required for customer-admin actions maybe.

[09:16] Lauren Bishop: That lines up with enterprise change control.

[09:19] Marissa Cole: Also we can show it in the audit trail view.

[09:23] Liam O'Rourke: Speaking of audit trail, did you find it? Did it help?

[09:27] Alex Chen: We found it but it was buried. Like we had to go to a different tab. I would expect it inline on the rollout page.

[09:36] Morgan Lee: Yeah, and I wanted to export it. Not sure if that’s in beta.

[09:41] Marissa Cole: Export is not fully there yet. We have internal-only right now.

[09:46] Samir Patel: Okay. Not a blocker but good to know.

[09:50] Liam O'Rourke: Let me check on the biggest pain: the thresholds being too strict. Would you say the top offenders were p99 and 429/drop?

[10:00] Priya Desai: Yes. And missing metrics.

[10:02] Alex Chen: Missing metrics behavior is the worst. Because if you don’t have the evidence, you fail. That means you can’t roll out during telemetry issues, which happens.

[10:12] Rafael Mendes: Our current philosophy is fail-closed for safety. But we are adding an explicit “stale metrics” indicator and letting admins override.

[10:22] Alex Chen: I get fail-closed. But can you distinguish “telemetry is down” from “actual bad metrics”? Because right now it’s the same red failed.

[10:30] Logan Wright: We can. We have staleness detection on the pipeline, like last sample timestamp. We just need to surface it.

[10:38] Marissa Cole: UI state could be “Unknown - telemetry delayed” and then action buttons change: you can hold, or force promote with warning.

[10:48] Alex Chen: That’d be better.

[10:50] Liam O'Rourke: On gate tuning: what would you prefer for the evaluation window? Right now we default 5 minutes for early stages.

[10:59] Priya Desai: For 1% we want 15 minutes or a minimum N requests. If it’s low traffic, time window doesn’t matter; we need sample size.

[11:09] Rafael Mendes: Yep. Minimum sample size is better.

[11:13] Lauren Bishop: We can add “must observe at least X requests” before evaluating latency percentiles.

[11:20] Alex Chen: Exactly.

[11:22] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s talk about the 429 situation from partitioning. If the canary stage gets effectively one replica, then queueing spikes. What’s the smallest stage you’d run?

[11:34] Alex Chen: Honestly? 10%. Or do replica-based partitioning: give canary at least 2 replicas. Percent-based on a small pool doesn’t work.

[11:44] Priya Desai: Our pool is 16 GPUs total. If canary is 1% you’re effectively below one GPU.

[11:50] Logan Wright: Right, fractional.

[11:52] Marissa Cole: Our UI should catch that and warn: “Stage may be too small for pool size.”

[11:58] Lauren Bishop: We have internal guidelines: minimum canary capacity. But we haven’t enforced it strongly because some customers asked for 1%.

[12:08] Alex Chen: I think 1% is fine for hosted, not for Dedicated partitioning.

[12:13] Liam O'Rourke: Got it.

[12:15] Samir Patel: I’d rather have a safe default and an override than let users shoot themselves.

[12:21] Lauren Bishop: Makes sense.

[12:23] Liam O'Rourke: Okay. Switching to UI asks. Marissa, do you want to ask specifics?

[12:29] Marissa Cole: Yeah. If you could change three things on the page, what are they?

[12:34] Alex Chen: One: show gate evidence, clearly. Like what query, what value, what threshold. Two: show region breakdown. Three: make hold/abort/rollback semantics obvious, including what happens to traffic.

[12:52] Priya Desai: Also: show a timeline with annotations. When did we promote, when did it fail, when did we hold. That’s in there but it’s not readable.

[13:02] Marissa Cole: Great.

[13:04] Morgan Lee: And maybe show the model version and runtime build right on the stage card. We had to click into config.

[13:11] Marissa Cole: Noted.

[13:13] Liam O'Rourke: On our side, we also want to ensure the system is reversible. Did you try rollback? How did it go?

[13:20] Alex Chen: We aborted. It reverted quickly.

[13:23] Priya Desai: But we don’t know what it did with in-flight requests. It’s not streaming so not huge, but we still want to know.

[13:32] Rafael Mendes: Today behavior is: in-flight requests stick to the selected partition; new requests follow the reverted routing policy. For streaming it’s pinned. We’ll document.

[13:44] Alex Chen: Okay.

[13:46] Liam O'Rourke: Another thing: do you want “auto promotion” at all? Or always manual?

[13:52] Samir Patel: Manual for now. Once we trust gates, auto promotion could be nice.

[13:58] Priya Desai: But auto rollback should be available. If error rate spikes, don’t wait for humans.

[14:04] Rafael Mendes: We agree. Auto abort on severe gates is what we’re targeting.

[14:11] Logan Wright: But we need to avoid false abort due to missing data.

[14:14] Priya Desai: Exactly.

[14:16] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s anchor: what are your “must have” gates, and which are “nice to have”?

[14:23] Priya Desai: Must have: 5xx error rate, 429/drop rate, and saturation/queue depth. Nice: p99 latency, OOM. Actually OOM is maybe must.

[14:36] Alex Chen: OOM is must. If the runtime OOMs we need to stop.

[14:40] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, OOM is a strong signal.

[14:43] Morgan Lee: For embeddings we also care about throughput, like tokens/sec, but I think that’s covered by saturation.

[14:50] Logan Wright: We can add throughput panels. But as a gate it’s tricky.

[14:56] Liam O'Rourke: Okay.

[14:58] Marissa Cole: On the gate evidence request: do you need raw PromQL queries or just the values and explanation?

[15:05] Alex Chen: Values and explanation. But having the query text is nice for debugging. You can tuck it under “advanced”.

[15:12] Priya Desai: Yeah. And show the time window and sampling, like last 5 min, last 15 min.

[15:18] Marissa Cole: Great.

[15:20] Liam O'Rourke: I want to check the earlier “force promote is scary” thing. What would make it less scary?

[15:27] Alex Chen: Two things: require an admin role and require a reason. And show a banner that says “gates are currently failing due to missing metrics” vs “gates failing due to actual p99”. Because in the missing metrics case we might accept.

[15:43] Rafael Mendes: That’s aligned with our guardrails.

[15:46] Lauren Bishop: Also possibly timebox it. Like force promote is allowed but then you must evaluate again at next stage.

[15:52] Priya Desai: Sure.

[15:54] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s jump to what you need from us in the next two weeks to continue beta.

[16:00] Alex Chen: Fix the evidence panel. And adjust the default stage sizes or warnings, so we don’t do 1% on a 16 GPU pool and get fake 429.

[16:12] Priya Desai: And change the p99 gate behavior. Make it relative or require sample size.

[16:18] Samir Patel: And general polish: don’t show red failure with no info.

[16:23] Liam O'Rourke: Understood.

[16:25] Logan Wright: For evidence panel, we can also send you the raw metrics externally while we fix UI, but long-term it should just work.

[16:33] Alex Chen: That would help. We can’t debug blind.

[16:37] Liam O'Rourke: Logan, can you pull the telemetry for Monday and confirm whether stage_id tags were missing?

[16:43] Logan Wright: Yep. If you send me the rollout ID, I can trace it.

[16:47] Alex Chen: I’ll send after the call.

[16:50] Liam O'Rourke: Great.

[16:52] Marissa Cole: Quick question: On the stage timeline, do you want the gates displayed per stage as a checklist, or a single consolidated “gate status” indicator?

[17:02] Priya Desai: Checklist. And show which one blocked promotion.

[17:06] Alex Chen: But don’t overwhelm. Group them: reliability, latency, capacity.

[17:12] Marissa Cole: That’s helpful.

[17:14] Liam O'Rourke: Any comments on naming? “Stage 1%, Stage 10%” etc.

[17:20] Morgan Lee: We’d like to rename: Canary, Ramp, Half, Full.

[17:24] Alex Chen: Yeah. And the UI should show both: Canary (10%).

[17:28] Lauren Bishop: Renaming is supported. We’ll ensure it’s obvious.

[17:32] Liam O'Rourke: Okay.

[17:34] Rafael Mendes: One more SRE angle: do you want promotions blocked if you’re in incident or high error budget burn?

[17:41] Priya Desai: Yes. 100%. If we’re burning budget we shouldn’t be making changes.

[17:46] Alex Chen: But again, show us why it’s blocked.

[17:49] Rafael Mendes: Yep.

[17:51] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Maybe we should talk about what thresholds feel right for you. I know you don’t want to pick numbers, but could you give rough ranges?

[18:00] Priya Desai: For error rate, if 5xx > 0.5% sustained, stop. For 429, if it’s above baseline by, like, 2x, stop. Latency: p95 can’t regress more than 20% maybe. p99… I don’t know, 50%? But only after enough samples.

[18:23] Alex Chen: And saturation: if queue depth p95 is above some number, or if we’re seeing GPU duty cycle pinned with increasing queue.

[18:32] Logan Wright: Great.

[18:34] Lauren Bishop: And do you want those to be configurable, or do you want Redwood defaults with maybe a small set of knobs?

[18:42] Samir Patel: Defaults are fine but allow override per pool. At least for p99 and 429.

[18:49] Alex Chen: Yes.

[18:51] Liam O'Rourke: Ok. We’re currently limiting custom metrics, but allowing threshold adjustment is on the roadmap.

[18:58] Alex Chen: That’s fine.

[19:00] (brief pause)

[19:02] Marissa Cole: Another UX thing: when a gate fails, do you want the system to recommend an action? Like “Hold and investigate” vs “Abort immediately”?

[19:12] Priya Desai: Yes but only if it’s confident. Like if it’s OOM, recommend abort.

[19:18] Alex Chen: If it’s missing data, recommend hold.

[19:21] Rafael Mendes: That’s reasonable.

[19:24] Liam O'Rourke: Switching gears—any mention internally of comparing this to other vendors? Just for our learning.

[19:31] Samir Patel: We looked at doing this ourselves on Kubernetes, or using AWS endpoints. But Dedicated with you is faster for us. We just need change control.

[19:41] Alex Chen: Together has a simple canary but not the gate evidence. OpenAI obviously isn’t Dedicated in the same way. So, you’re ahead, but the UX needs to match the promise.

[19:55] Liam O'Rourke: Appreciate that.

[19:57] Lauren Bishop: One more: on stage promotion, would you prefer percent-based always, or replica-based for Dedicated?

[20:04] Alex Chen: Replica-based. Or hybrid: user chooses percent, but the system enforces minimum replica count.

[20:12] Priya Desai: Yes. Minimum 2 replicas for canary.

[20:16] Lauren Bishop: Okay.

[20:18] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s do a recap soon. Anything else you want to flag? Especially any confusing copy.

[20:25] Morgan Lee: The “abort” button said something like “abort rollout plan”. We weren’t sure if it deletes it or just stops it.

[20:32] Marissa Cole: Good catch. It just stops and reverts, but does not delete. We can change the text to “Abort and revert” and keep a “Delete plan” separately.

[20:43] Alex Chen: Yeah.

[20:45] Priya Desai: Also the gate names: “request drop” vs “429”. We want it consistent with our language.

[20:52] Rafael Mendes: We can map them.

[20:54] Liam O'Rourke: Ok.

[20:56] Liam O'Rourke: I’m going to do a quick summary of what I heard and you tell me if I missed anything.

[21:02] Liam O'Rourke: 1) Evidence panel needs to show actual values/thresholds and not be blank; include “unknown due to telemetry lag” as distinct state.
2) Default stages: 1% is too small for Dedicated partitioning; we should enforce min capacity or warn; support replica-minimum.
3) Gates: p99 latency should be sample-size aware and ideally relative-to-baseline; 429 gate should consider baseline and stage size.
4) UI: make hold semantics explicit (traffic stays at current stage), clarify rollback/abort language, add per-region breakdown and better timeline annotations.
5) Change control: require reason/change ticket for promote and force-promote; keep admin-only override.

[21:41] Alex Chen: Yep, that’s accurate.

[21:44] Priya Desai: +1.

[21:46] Marissa Cole: Great.

[21:48] Logan Wright: I’ll follow up for rollout ID.

[21:51] Liam O'Rourke: Perfect. Next steps: we’ll send proposed threshold changes and UI changes. We can also schedule a follow-up once the evidence panel fix is out.

[22:02] Samir Patel: Sounds good.

[22:05] Liam O'Rourke: Anything else before we end?

[22:09] Alex Chen: Just one question: when will “customer configurable thresholds” be available? Even limited.

[22:16] Lauren Bishop: Not in the next two weeks, but in the next phase of beta we can allow per-pool overrides for a small subset—latency and 429. We’ll keep you posted.

[22:27] Alex Chen: Ok.

[22:29] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Thanks everyone. We’ll send notes. Recording off.

[22:34] (end)

Note: Some transcript timestamps may be compressed due to Fireflies processing; speaker attribution may be imperfect in a few segments.

title: Dedicated Beta - Staged Rollouts check-in (gates + Console feedback)
preview: Beta check-in with Lattice AI on Dedicated staged rollouts. Customer likes the stage timeline concept and promotion audit trail, but reported friction around (1) gate strictness (p99 latency + 429/drop thresholds) and noisy failures during low-traffic periods, (2) confusion about what "saturation/headroom" means per stage, and (3) Console UX gaps: unclear gate evidence, limited per-region breakdown, and too many clicks to promote/hold/rollback. Redwood aligned on tuning: minimum sample size, better missing-metrics messaging, and UI improvements (gate evidence panel, annotations, and simplified promote confirmation).
text: Beta check-in with Lattice AI on Dedicated staged rollouts. Customer likes the stage timeline concept and promotion audit trail, but reported friction around (1) gate strictness (p99 latency + 429/drop thresholds) and noisy failures during low-traffic periods, (2) confusion about what "saturation/headroom" means per stage, and (3) Console UX gaps: unclear gate evidence, limited per-region breakdown, and too many clicks to promote/hold/rollback. Redwood aligned on tuning: minimum sample size, better missing-metrics messaging, and UI improvements (gate evidence panel, annotations, and simplified promote confirmation).

source.title: Dedicated upgrade safety – staged rollouts / gates / rollback semantics (technical deep dive)
topics: Staged rollouts for Dedicated reserved pools (traffic % vs replica partitions)
Missing metrics behavior (fail-closed vs fail-open) and manual overrides
Rollback/abort semantics, especially for streaming requests
Multi-region Dedicated pools and region impairment during a stage
Audit logging, RBAC, and change management expectations
next_steps: Redwood to share draft gate defaults (p95/p99, 5m vs 15m windows, min sample size) and what is configurable in v1.
Customer to provide current SLOs and peak/burst traffic characteristics (tokens/sec, concurrency) for sizing and stage planning.
Redwood to send a short write-up on small-pool partitioning guardrails (minimum replicas/headroom) and expected 429 behavior protections.
Schedule a follow-up: Console walkthrough of stage timeline + gate evidence panel (once available) with Arjun/Nina.
action_items: Paula Mendes - Send proposed default gate thresholds + windowing/min-sample guidance for Dedicated staged rollouts (ETA: 2025-02-10).
Ethan Park - Provide API-level semantics doc: promote/abort/hold + idempotency + audit event examples (ETA: 2025-02-12).
Mina Haddad - Share routing/partitioning note: replica partitions vs % traffic, handling multi-region pools, and fallback behavior on impairment (ETA: 2025-02-12).
Nina Patel (Lattice AI) - Share pool size assumptions and whether reservations are single-region or multi-region; include minimum acceptable canary size (ETA: 2025-02-11).
Arjun Mehta (Lattice AI) - Provide historical incident examples (429 spikes, OOMs, GPU node issues) and desired rollback runbook steps (ETA: 2025-02-14).
source.summary: Lattice AI asked for a detailed walkthrough of how Redwood will do safer Dedicated pool upgrades using staged rollouts. Redwood described the rollout plan model (stages, gates, promote/abort/hold) and emphasized deterministic, auditable actions with automatic abort/rollback on gate failure. The group focused on how stages map to reserved capacity (traffic splitting vs replica partitions), avoiding starving small pools during canary, and how to handle missing/noisy metrics. Lattice AI emphasized strict change management (audit logs, RBAC, reason codes), and asked about rollback semantics for streaming and multi-region behavior if one region degrades mid-stage.
source.transcript:
Meeting: Dedicated upgrade safety – staged rollouts / gates / rollback semantics (technical deep dive)
Date: 2025-02-06
Time: 2:05pm – 2:58pm PT
Duration: 53 minutes
Attendees (Redwood): Liam O'Rourke, Mina Haddad, Ethan Park, Rafael Mendes, Paula Mendes
Attendees (Lattice AI): Nina Patel, Arjun Mehta, Jules Kim, Dana Wu

---

[00:00] Liam O'Rourke: Alright, I think we're mostly all here. Nina, Arjun, Jules, Dana—good to see you. Goal today is go deeper than the overview deck, specifically on staged rollouts for Dedicated pools. Mina and Ethan can get into routing and control plane details, Rafael can sanity check with SRE hat, and Paula is here for the gate policy side.

[00:24] Nina Patel: Great. Yeah, we had a pretty bad experience last year upgrading a serving stack where the “canary” basically just broke the whole pool. So we want to be extremely specific about how your canary works when it’s reserved capacity.

[00:38] Mina Haddad: Yup, makes sense. Dedicated is a different beast because you can’t just send 1% of traffic if that 1% maps to like… one GPU, and then that GPU is saturated and you start 429’ing.

[00:49] Arjun Mehta: Exactly. Our reservations are not huge. Like sometimes it’s 8 GPUs in a region for a given model, and if you split that wrong it’s basically guaranteed pain.

[01:02] Liam O'Rourke: Maybe quick framing: we’re talking about staged rollouts as a mechanism to upgrade model versions, runtime builds, or routing policies within your Dedicated pool. This is not “experimentation platform” broadly, it’s safety rollouts.

[01:17] Paula Mendes: And each stage has “gates,” meaning objective signals that must pass before you can promote to the next stage. Some can be automated and some can be manual sign-off depending on your config and the release.

[01:31] Jules Kim: Can you say what the stage actually is. Is it percent traffic? Is it replica count? We care because our workload isn’t uniform.

[01:40] Ethan Park: Sure. In the control plane we represent a rollout as a plan against a Dedicated pool: a list of ordered stages. Each stage defines an allocation to the “new” version. Allocation can be expressed as percentage, but internally we can map it to partitions/replicas depending on pool size.

[01:58] Mina Haddad: Yeah, practically: for larger pools we can do traffic percentages with good smoothing. For small pools, we push you toward replica partitions: e.g., “1 replica canary” rather than “1% traffic.” Because 1% traffic doesn’t mean anything if the request router can’t maintain fairness.

[02:18] Nina Patel: So it’s deterministic? Like stage 1 equals exactly N GPUs running the new build?

[02:24] Mina Haddad: That’s the idea. There are two “dimensions” depending on how your pool is deployed. If it’s a single regional pool, we can allocate N replicas in that region to the new version. If it’s multi-region, we allocate per region with constraints so you don’t end up with the new version only in one region unless you ask for that.

[02:46] Arjun Mehta: Okay. And the router—does it do per-request decision, or per-session, or what? We stream responses, sometimes long.

[02:56] Mina Haddad: Good question. Today we treat each request independently, but streaming complicates rollback. We’re adding semantics where in-flight streaming requests are pinned to the backend they started on. If you abort a stage, we stop sending new requests to the canary partition, but we let in-flight streams complete unless you explicitly “drain/terminate.”

[03:19] Rafael Mendes: From SRE perspective, terminating in-flight streams on rollback is usually worse because you create customer-visible errors. So default is drain.

[03:31] Dana Wu: That impacts audit and change management. If you “rollback” but there’s residual traffic for minutes, we need that documented.

[03:39] Ethan Park: Totally. The state machine will record: abort initiated at time X, routing changed at time X+delta, and then a drain completion marker once the system sees no in-flight sessions on the canary partition (with a timeout). All of those events are audit logged.

[04:02] Nina Patel: Can you talk about “hold.” Like we need the ability to freeze mid-stage.

[04:08] Paula Mendes: Hold is basically a pause: you do not promote and you do not automatically rollback. It’s a stable state where gates keep evaluating but we do not take action automatically. It’s for human investigation. We also recommend an auto-timeout so you don’t leave a pool permanently in a partial state.

[04:28] Arjun Mehta: What’s the default action on gate failure? Auto-abort?

[04:33] Rafael Mendes: In most safety rollouts, yes. If a required gate fails, the system should abort and route back to the baseline partition. There are guardrails because if your baseline is already burning error budget, you may not even want to start a rollout.

[04:51] Nina Patel: That’s interesting—so you block promotion if baseline is unhealthy?

[04:55] Paula Mendes: We’re aligning gate policy with SLOs. For example: if your pool is already above a certain error budget burn rate over the last N minutes, promotions are disabled (or require override). The idea is “no changes during incident.”

[05:13] Jules Kim: What are the actual signals. Like list them.

[05:17] Paula Mendes: For v1: request error rate (5xx + model execution errors), latency percentiles (p95/p99), saturation/headroom proxies (GPU utilization, queue depth), OOM rate, request drops/429, and GPU health signals like node taints/ECC errors where available.

[05:41] Arjun Mehta: You said 429. That’s the one we’re terrified about. If you carve out a canary that’s too small, it will 429 and then your system thinks the canary is “bad,” but it was just too small.

[05:55] Mina Haddad: Exactly. That’s why we have minimum partition sizing guidelines and stage validation. The control plane rejects a plan that would allocate less than, say, one full replica—or less than a minimum headroom target—based on your pool size and model profile.

[06:13] Ethan Park: There’s also monotonicity validation: stages must increase allocation. And we validate that baseline partition doesn’t fall below safe operational capacity.

[06:24] Nina Patel: What’s “safe operational capacity”? Is that a fixed number?

[06:29] Rafael Mendes: It’s not one fixed number. It’s a combination: you need enough baseline capacity to keep serving at your expected load plus burst. If you have an 8 GPU pool and you take 1 GPU for canary, that’s a 12.5% reduction in baseline. If your baseline already runs at 80-85% at peak, you’re in trouble.

[06:52] Mina Haddad: We can enforce “must maintain X% headroom” using observed saturation metrics. But early in stage 0, we may not have enough data. So we also use static minimums: don’t partition below certain replica counts for certain model classes.

[07:11] Jules Kim: So does that mean if my pool is small, I just can’t do a canary?

[07:16] Mina Haddad: You can, but the canary might be “1 replica” and you might also need temporary burst capacity or a shorter canary window with manual observation. For really small pools, sometimes the safest path is “upgrade in a shadow pool” then cutover. That’s out of scope for v1, but we can talk operationally.

[07:39] Nina Patel: We can sometimes add burst capacity but it takes time. How fast can you add GPUs to a Dedicated reservation mid-rollout?

[07:47] Liam O'Rourke: Commercially, we can do burst add-ons for some customers. Mechanically, it depends on region availability and your contract. Irene/Vanessa on our side own that, but we can follow up.

[08:02] Arjun Mehta: Missing metrics. If the telemetry pipeline is down, what happens? Because we’ve been burned where the system is like “unknown” and just sits.

[08:11] Paula Mendes: This is a big debate internally too. Current stance for safety rollouts is “fail-closed” for required gates: if the metric is missing or stale beyond a staleness threshold, the gate does not pass. That blocks promotion, and depending on configuration can trigger abort if we believe the system can’t safely validate.

[08:36] Dana Wu: That’s better for compliance but could cause a lot of churn if your observability has a hiccup.

[08:42] Rafael Mendes: We’re trying to be practical. We won’t auto-abort just because one metric is missing for 30 seconds. There’s staleness detection and grace windows. But we will not automatically promote into uncertainty.

[08:57] Arjun Mehta: And can an operator override?

[09:00] Ethan Park: Yes, but RBAC guarded. Force-promote and force-rollback/abort are privileged actions. They require a reason string, and we emit audit events with old stage/new stage, actor identity, and the “override=true” flag.

[09:18] Dana Wu: Can the customer admin see those override events?

[09:22] Liam O'Rourke: For enterprise accounts, yes, the audit log is exportable. There may be some internal-only fields, but the action, actor type, and reason will be visible. We can align exactly what you need.

[09:37] Nina Patel: Another question. If stage 1 is 10% traffic, what does that mean if traffic is spiky? We do bursty batch jobs.

[09:46] Mina Haddad: In routing, we can do consistent hashing on a request key if you have one (like tenant ID) so the same tenant sticks to the same partition. If not, it’s per-request. Spiky batch jobs are harder because 10% by request count can still be 50% of tokens. So we prefer replica partitions and token-aware load balancing.

[10:11] Jules Kim: We have tenant IDs, but some tenants do both chat and long-form.

[10:16] Mina Haddad: Right. We can hash on tenant for fairness, but we still monitor token throughput and queue depth. Gate windows can be longer (like 15m) for bursty workloads so you have enough sample.

[10:34] Paula Mendes: There’s also a “minimum sample size” gate. Like don’t evaluate p99 if you only have 200 requests. You end up with noise.

[10:46] Arjun Mehta: What are you using as default window? 5 minutes? 15?

[10:51] Paula Mendes: We’re leaning 10-15 for some gates, with a shorter window for fast-failing signals like error spikes and OOM. But we’ll share the draft. It’s one of the open questions.

[11:07] Nina Patel: Ok, can we walk through a concrete scenario? We want to upgrade from runtime build A to build B. Stage plan: 1 replica, then 2, then 50%, then 100. What exactly happens.

[11:20] Ethan Park: Step by step:
- Create rollout plan in draft with stages defined.
- Start rollout: system deploys build B to canary partition (stage 1) and shifts routing accordingly.
- Gate evaluation runs continuously for the stage window.
- If gates pass and an operator (or automation) promotes, we move to stage 2: increase partition allocation.
- If any required gate fails, we abort: routing reverts to baseline partition; canary replicas are drained and then scaled down.

[11:55] Rafael Mendes: Also, we annotate these changes in dashboards so oncall can correlate “promotion at 14:32” with metric changes.

[12:04] Arjun Mehta: And does abort automatically roll back the runtime build? Like, do you delete it.

[12:09] Mina Haddad: We keep it around for a bit for forensics, but it’s out of rotation. Exact retention depends; we don’t want to burn GPU on a bad build.

[12:20] Jules Kim: What about model version upgrades. Those are scary because of output changes. Are you gating on quality?

[12:27] Paula Mendes: In v1, quality gating is limited. We can integrate with your eval harness if it’s available, but generally we treat quality as a “manual gate” initially. Long term we want automated regression checks tied to prompt sets.

[12:45] Nina Patel: We do have an internal eval suite. It’s not perfect.

[12:49] Liam O'Rourke: We can start with: during stage 1, you run your eval suite against the canary endpoint and then approve promotion.

[13:00] Dana Wu: Is that recorded anywhere? For audit we need “who approved.”

[13:04] Ethan Park: Yes, manual gates are explicit actions. You click “approve” or submit approval via API; it becomes an audit event.

[13:15] Arjun Mehta: Multi-region. We might do active-active between us-west and us-east. If you canary only in one region, that region might look worse, then you abort, but actually it was a region issue.

[13:31] Mina Haddad: Great point. For multi-region pools we evaluate gates per region and globally. If one region is impaired, we can either hold promotion, or we can route away from the impaired region and continue evaluation in the healthy region, depending on policy.

[13:52] Rafael Mendes: SRE stance: if there’s a region incident, you should probably hold. Changes during incident are risky. But there are escape hatches.

[14:02] Nina Patel: We’d probably want “hold on regional impairment.”

[14:06] Paula Mendes: We can configure “no promote during incident” by checking incident flags or elevated burn.

[14:14] Jules Kim: You mentioned GPU health signals. How real-time are those. Like ECC errors and throttling—those can be flaky.

[14:22] Rafael Mendes: They’re not perfect, but we can use them as “warning gates” rather than hard fail, or require multiple samples. Also we only use allowlisted labels to avoid cardinality blowups.

[14:38] Arjun Mehta: Speaking of cardinality, are you tagging metrics by stage? Because that gets wild.

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: Yes, but carefully. Stage_id is low cardinality per pool. We do not tag by request id obviously. There are guardrails.

[14:56] Mina Haddad: You’ll see dashboards: baseline vs canary partition, plus stage timeline annotations.

[15:04] Nina Patel: Ok. Now the canary-starves-baseline issue. Let’s say we do stage 1 with 1 GPU. That GPU saturates, canary errors spike, gate fails, and you abort. But the baseline also saw latency increase because routing kept trying to send to canary, then retry? How do you avoid that spiral.

[15:26] Mina Haddad: Two things:
1) We don’t do naive retries that double load. Retry policies are bounded, and we can route retries back to baseline only if the canary is unhealthy.
2) We have “capacity starvation protection”: if canary queue depth exceeds a threshold, we temporarily reduce traffic to canary even if the target is 10%, basically a circuit breaker.

[15:53] Arjun Mehta: So you violate the stage allocation target in order to protect SLO.

[15:58] Mina Haddad: Correct. Safety first. That also becomes a signal—if the system had to clamp canary traffic due to saturation, the stage probably shouldn’t be promoted.

[16:11] Paula Mendes: That can be a gate: “no sustained clamp events” or “canary saturation under threshold.”

[16:18] Nina Patel: That’s good. But we’ll need transparency in the UI: it can’t just be “gate failed.” We need why.

[16:26] Ethan Park: Gate evidence will include the query, time window, observed value, threshold, and pass/fail reasoning. Not perfect in v1, but that’s the intent.

[16:41] Dana Wu: And do we get an API to export that evidence? We archive change tickets.

[16:46] Ethan Park: Yes, evidence retrieval is an endpoint. Also audit events include pointers to evaluation snapshots.

[16:57] Liam O'Rourke: Maybe quick pause—does this map to what you’re looking for? Or are we missing a big requirement.

[17:03] Nina Patel: This is on track. Our main requirement is: no more “big bang” upgrades, and our SREs need deterministic rollback with a clear runbook.

[17:13] Arjun Mehta: And we need to know what happens if a stage gets stuck. Like metrics missing, or gate is flapping.

[17:20] Rafael Mendes: Stuck stage: you can hold, then investigate. Gate flapping: we can require sustained pass, e.g. pass for 3 consecutive evaluations, and we can use longer windows. Also, we alert but we try not to page on every blip.

[17:39] Jules Kim: Alerts—who gets paged when a gate fails? Us or you.

[17:44] Liam O'Rourke: For Dedicated, it depends on support model. Generally Redwood oncall gets notified for platform-level issues, and your operators see it in Console. For your self-managed rollouts, you may want your oncall to get a webhook.

[18:01] Ethan Park: We can integrate with your alerting via webhook in later versions; for now, we have console + email notifications and internal paging.

[18:14] Nina Patel: Ok. Can we talk about RBAC? Who can force-promote.

[18:18] Ethan Park: Force-promote is admin-only. In Redwood terms, customer “Org Admin” may not be enough; it’s a special permission. You can grant it to a subset. And we can require a second approver—policy option—though that might be later.

[18:39] Dana Wu: We’d want at least “reason required” and audit retention.

[18:44] Liam O'Rourke: Audit retention is aligned with enterprise defaults. We can share the retention policy separately.

[18:52] Arjun Mehta: I want to push on one detail. Fail-closed on missing metrics sounds good, but what about if your telemetry pipeline lags by like 3 minutes. Then it looks missing and you block promotion even though everything is fine.

[19:08] Paula Mendes: That’s why we have staleness thresholds, not “missing instantly.” For example, if data is older than 2x the evaluation period, we treat it as stale. We’re tuning those values. There’s also UI messaging: “Gate unknown due to stale telemetry,” not “failed.”

[19:28] Rafael Mendes: And we wouldn’t auto-abort just because telemetry is stale unless policy says so. Most likely we’d hold.

[19:37] Nina Patel: Good, because otherwise you can get in loops.

[19:42] Jules Kim: On abort, do you always revert to previous stage or always to baseline.

[19:47] Mina Haddad: Abort means revert to baseline (stage 0). Rollback can also mean “step back one stage” but we’re trying to keep semantics simple. The safest is go back to last known good baseline.

[20:02] Ethan Park: We might support “demote one stage” later, but v1 is baseline.

[20:10] Arjun Mehta: Okay. Another scenario: We promote to 50%, everything looks good, then at 100% we see a rare error. Do you automatically roll back to baseline or do you only block further actions.

[20:24] Paula Mendes: If the gate is required and it fails at stage N, we abort back to baseline. But there’s nuance: some issues only appear at scale. So yes, it would abort, but you’d want to capture evidence.

[20:39] Rafael Mendes: From incident management angle, auto-abort is usually better than sitting at 100% with elevated errors. But if baseline is also bad or you need to keep the new version, then you force-hold.

[20:56] Dana Wu: All of this needs to be in the audit trail.

[20:59] Ethan Park: Yep.

[21:03] Liam O'Rourke: We’re at about 20 minutes. Mina, can you explain how partitioning works in more detail? Like how we avoid fragmentation.

[21:10] Mina Haddad: Sure. A Dedicated pool is a set of reserved GPUs (or “replicas” of the runtime) behind a router. During staged rollout we logically partition that pool into baseline and canary partitions. Each partition runs a specific “target” (model version + runtime build + config). We don’t want too many partitions because you fragment capacity. So in v1 it’s basically two partitions: current and new.

[21:39] Nina Patel: Only two? That’s good.

[21:41] Mina Haddad: Stages are about how much traffic/capacity goes to the new partition over time, not about creating multiple partitions.

[21:51] Arjun Mehta: That’s an important detail. So stage 1, 2, 3 are just changing weights between two partitions.

[21:58] Mina Haddad: Exactly.

[22:00] Jules Kim: What about if you want A/B between two new versions?

[22:04] Liam O'Rourke: Not in v1. This is rollout safety. A/B beyond that is later.

[22:12] Nina Patel: For multi-region, are partitions per region or global.

[22:18] Mina Haddad: Per region, because the underlying fleet is region-scoped. But the plan can specify “keep weights consistent across regions” or “regional canary.” Most customers want consistent to avoid split-brain.

[22:35] Rafael Mendes: And if one region lacks enough replicas to allocate the canary, we fail validation or we adjust. We won’t partially canary one region unless policy says so.

[22:48] Arjun Mehta: That’s good. We had a vendor where canary was only in us-east and it made troubleshooting miserable.

[22:56] Nina Patel: Ok can we talk about how you choose the canary traffic. Like if we say 10%, does router enforce exactly 10%.

[23:05] Mina Haddad: It’s approximate, with smoothing. For tenant-hash it’s closer to deterministic by tenant count, not request count. For per-request it’s statistical. But again, for small pools we recommend replica-based.

[23:24] Jules Kim: For replica-based, how do you ensure the canary replica isn’t overloaded.

[23:29] Mina Haddad: You can’t guarantee it if traffic is not controllable, but you can use routing keys to distribute and you can cap canary traffic. Also stage 1 is usually very short, with a “max queue depth” breaker.

[23:46] Arjun Mehta: That cap—is it hard?

[23:49] Mina Haddad: Yes, it’s like: if canary is saturated, we stop sending additional traffic and route to baseline.

[23:58] Paula Mendes: And that saturation event gets recorded.

[24:02] Nina Patel: So that avoids 429s.

[24:05] Mina Haddad: It reduces them. If canary is already full you might still get a few 429s depending on concurrency, but baseline should be protected.

[24:17] Rafael Mendes: We also gate on request drop/429; if you see 429s in canary, that’s a likely fail unless it’s explained by too-small stage.

[24:29] Nina Patel: That’s where we’d want the system to say “this canary is undersized,” not “new build is bad.”

[24:36] Paula Mendes: Exactly. That’s why we want both absolute 429 and “clamp event count” signals.

[24:44] Dana Wu: I’m going to ask a compliance-ish question. When you change routing weights, is that a config change. Is it versioned.

[24:53] Ethan Park: Yes. Rollout plan references specific artifacts (model version, runtime build, config hash). Each promotion step is recorded with before/after values. We also keep optimistic concurrency controls to prevent double-promote under retries.

[25:12] Arjun Mehta: Idempotency keys.

[25:14] Ethan Park: Exactly. Promote/abort endpoints accept idempotency keys.

[25:20] Nina Patel: Great.

[25:22] Liam O'Rourke: I’m going to jump to: what do you need from us to get comfortable? Like what would you want to see in a pilot.

[25:31] Nina Patel: We want a runbook, and we want to run one upgrade on a non-critical pool first.

[25:37] Arjun Mehta: And we want to see the gate evaluation. Like show us the actual query windows and the thresholds. We’ve seen “magic scoring” systems and we don’t trust them.

[25:47] Paula Mendes: Totally. We can share the draft and you can review. We’re trying to keep it transparent.

[25:54] Jules Kim: Also “quality.” Even if v1 is manual, we need a pattern.

[26:01] Liam O'Rourke: That’s doable. We can integrate manual approval step tied to your eval run.

[26:09] Dana Wu: And audit export. We need to export events for our change management system.

[26:14] Ethan Park: Yes.

[26:16] (crosstalk)

[26:17] Mina Haddad: Sorry, one more thing on rollback semantics: when you abort, new requests route to baseline immediately. In-flight pinned streams drain. If the baseline is incompatible (rare), we can also route to fallback region if configured.

[26:33] Arjun Mehta: Is fallback region a thing for Dedicated? Usually Dedicated means we’re pinned.

[26:39] Mina Haddad: Typically pinned, yes. But some customers have a secondary pool or an overflow to hosted. That’s more advanced.

[26:48] Rafael Mendes: We don’t want to surprise you by failing over to hosted if you didn’t agree.

[26:53] Nina Patel: Yeah no surprises.

[26:56] Liam O'Rourke: We’re aligned.

[27:01] Nina Patel: Ok, last area: how do you avoid “over-partitioning.” If we have 8 GPUs and we do stage 1, 2, 3, are you redeploying each time.

[27:12] Mina Haddad: We scale the canary partition up/down as needed. The plan validations ensure we don’t create too many small slices. But scaling up can take a little time depending on warmup.

[27:26] Jules Kim: Warmup matters. Cold start can cause latency spikes.

[27:30] Mina Haddad: Yep. We can have a warmup gate: don’t start evaluation until the replica has served some minimal traffic or has warmed caches.

[27:41] Paula Mendes: Or “exclude first N minutes” from gate evaluation. We do that in some systems.

[27:47] Arjun Mehta: That’s good. We’ve seen false fails from cold caches.

[27:53] Rafael Mendes: Also if there’s a KV cache warmup effect, you might want to do a short ramp rather than instantaneous.

[28:02] Nina Patel: Ok. I think this covers it.

[28:06] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s do a quick recap and next steps. Paula will send the proposed gate defaults and windowing. Ethan will send the API semantics and audit payload examples. Mina will send a note on partitioning/small pool guardrails and multi-region behavior.

[28:25] Nina Patel: And we’ll send you our current pool sizing and SLO targets.

[28:29] Arjun Mehta: Plus some incident examples. Especially around 429 spikes.

[28:34] Dana Wu: And I’ll follow up with what we need in audit exports (fields, retention).

[28:40] Liam O'Rourke: Perfect. We’ll schedule a follow-up maybe next week for a Console workflow walkthrough, even if it’s early screenshots.

[28:49] Nina Patel: Sounds good.

[28:51] Liam O'Rourke: Thanks everyone.

---
Notes: Transcript generated by Fireflies.ai; may contain transcription errors (e.g., “KV cash” vs KV cache, speaker attribution).

Redwood described the rollout plan model (stages, gates, promote/abort/hold) and emphasized deterministic, auditable actions with automatic abort/rollback on gate failure. The group focused on how stages map to reserved capacity (traffic splitting vs replica partitions), avoiding starving small pools during canary, and how to handle missing/noisy metrics.
